adjunct.name: Hydration and Electrolyte Support
adjunct.description: Structured hydration habits and electrolyte replacement when appetite, intake, vomiting, or diarrhea reduce fluid balance.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Semaglutide labeling identifies GI adverse effects and acute kidney injury due to volume depletion; NIDDK constipation guidance supports fluids with fiber.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Avoid aggressive electrolyte loading when fluid, sodium, potassium, or kidney restrictions are medically directed.

citationRefs.title: DailyMed WEGOVY semaglutide prescribing information
Eating, Diet, and Nutrition for Constipation - NIDDK

rationale: Reduced appetite, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and constipation can lower fluid intake and increase dehydration risk.
expectedBenefit: Supports hydration habits and constipation prevention without adding another compound.
safetyCaveats: Escalating GI symptoms, persistent vomiting, severe constipation, dizziness, or suspected dehydration require clinician review.
avoidIf: Fluid restriction, severe kidney disease, heart failure fluid limits, or clinician-directed electrolyte restrictions.
implementationNotes: Supportive context only; not a peptide dose recommendation.

Eating, Diet, and Nutrition for Constipation - NIDDK

safetyCaveats: Escalating GI symptoms, persistent vomiting, severe constipation, dizziness, or suspected dehydration require clinician review.

adjunct.name: Hydration and Electrolyte Support
adjunct.description: Structured hydration habits and electrolyte replacement when appetite, intake, vomiting, or diarrhea reduce fluid balance.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Cagrilintide and semaglutide trials report GI adverse events; NIDDK constipation guidance supports fluids with fiber.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Avoid aggressive electrolyte loading when fluid, sodium, potassium, or kidney restrictions are medically directed.

citationRefs.title: Coadministered Cagrilintide and Semaglutide in Adults with Overweight or Obesity
Eating, Diet, and Nutrition for Constipation - NIDDK

rationale: Dual amylin and GLP-1 signaling can intensify satiety and GI adverse effects, making hydration and bowel-habit monitoring especially relevant.
expectedBenefit: Helps users notice fluid-intake, constipation, and volume-depletion risks while avoiding another active compound.
safetyCaveats: Persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, severe constipation, or inability to maintain fluids requires clinician review.
avoidIf: Fluid restriction, severe kidney disease, heart failure fluid limits, or clinician-directed electrolyte restrictions.
implementationNotes: Supportive context only; not a peptide dose recommendation.

adjunct.name: Protein and Resistance Training
adjunct.description: Adequate protein intake plus progressive resistance training to protect lean mass during pharmacologic weight loss.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Reviews and expert consensus on incretin-based therapy emphasize resistance training, adequate protein, and body-composition monitoring for muscle preservation.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Training should be scaled to injury status, frailty, cardiometabolic risk, and clinician or coach guidance.

citationRefs.title: Incretin-Based Weight Loss Pharmacotherapy: Can Resistance Exercise Optimize Changes in Body Composition?
Nutritional and lifestyle supportive care recommendations for management of obesity with GLP-1-based therapies

rationale: Rapid appetite reduction can reduce total protein intake and weight loss can include lean mass loss.
expectedBenefit: Supports body-composition quality, strength retention, and weight-maintenance resilience during therapy.
safetyCaveats: New or intensive training should be scaled for orthopedic limits, frailty, cardiovascular disease, and recovery capacity.
avoidIf: Unstable cardiac symptoms, acute injury, severe illness, or clinician-advised exercise restriction.
implementationNotes: Supportive lifestyle context only; does not define a nutrition or training prescription.

adjunct.name: Protein and Resistance Training
adjunct.description: Adequate protein intake plus progressive resistance training to protect lean mass during pharmacologic weight loss.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Reviews and expert consensus on incretin-based therapy emphasize resistance training, adequate protein, and body-composition monitoring for muscle preservation.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Training should be scaled to injury status, frailty, cardiometabolic risk, and clinician or coach guidance.

citationRefs.title: Incretin-Based Weight Loss Pharmacotherapy: Can Resistance Exercise Optimize Changes in Body Composition?
Nutritional and lifestyle supportive care recommendations for management of obesity with GLP-1-based therapies

rationale: Large weight reductions can include lean mass loss, especially when appetite suppression lowers protein intake.
expectedBenefit: Supports body-composition quality, strength retention, and weight-maintenance resilience during therapy.
safetyCaveats: New or intensive training should be scaled for orthopedic limits, frailty, cardiovascular disease, and recovery capacity.
avoidIf: Unstable cardiac symptoms, acute injury, severe illness, or clinician-advised exercise restriction.
implementationNotes: Supportive lifestyle context only; does not define a nutrition or training prescription.

adjunct.name: Trimethylglycine Methylation Support
adjunct.description: Betaine or trimethylglycine context for methyl-donor support when NAD precursor use raises methylation questions.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Betaine participates in homocysteine remethylation; NR methylation literature shows methyl-dependent NAM metabolite handling but does not prove routine TMG is required.
adjunct.safetyNotes: This is a mechanistic support concept, not a requirement. Review homocysteine, methylation history, and supplement tolerance before use.

citationRefs.title: Betaine - LiverTox - NCBI Bookshelf
Betaine supplementation decreases plasma homocysteine in healthy adult participants: a meta-analysis
Nicotinamide riboside supplementation is not associated with altered methylation homeostasis in Parkinson's disease

benefitGoal: methylation context for NAD precursor use
rationale: Nicotinamide handling can involve methyl-dependent metabolites, while betaine provides an alternate remethylation pathway for homocysteine.
expectedBenefit: Frames TMG as a candidate support when methylation labs, homocysteine, or clinician review indicate a need.
safetyCaveats: Do not assume methyl donors are universally beneficial; excess or poorly tolerated methyl donors can worsen symptoms in susceptible users.
avoidIf: Clinician-advised methyl donor avoidance, unstable psychiatric symptoms, supplement intolerance, or unmanaged kidney disease.
implementationNotes: Mechanistic context only; not a supplement prescription.

adjunct.name: Topical Hyaluronic Acid Hydration Support
adjunct.description: Topical hyaluronic-acid skin hydration context for cosmetic or dermal barrier goals.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Hyaluronic acid is a key skin-moisture molecule, and topical/oral trials show hydration and barrier-function signals in selected populations.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Patch-test sensitive skin and avoid applying irritating products to broken or infected skin unless directed by a clinician.

citationRefs.title: Hyaluronic acid: A key molecule in skin aging
Effectiveness of topical hyaluronic acid of different molecular weights in xerosis cutis treatment in elderly

rationale: GHK-Cu is framed around dermal remodeling, while hyaluronic acid specifically supports water-binding and barrier hydration.
expectedBenefit: Complements dermal peptide goals with non-peptide hydration support.
safetyCaveats: Topical irritation, fragrance sensitivity, or barrier disruption can occur with unsuitable formulations.
avoidIf: Known sensitivity to the formulation, active skin infection, or open wounds without clinician guidance.
implementationNotes: Supportive skincare context only; not an injectable protocol.

adjunct.name: Circadian Light Timing
adjunct.description: Consistent wake time, morning light exposure, and evening light reduction as circadian support context.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: AASM and NHLBI resources support appropriately timed light therapy for selected circadian rhythm disorders.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Bright light timing can be counterproductive in some circadian disorders and should be personalized for severe sleep-wake disruption.

citationRefs.title: AASM clinical guideline for circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders
Circadian Rhythm Disorders - Treatment - NHLBI

rationale: Epitalon is often discussed in pineal and circadian contexts, but light-dark timing is the foundational non-compound circadian signal.
expectedBenefit: Supports sleep-wake regularity without adding another compound.
safetyCaveats: Incorrect light timing may worsen delayed or non-24 sleep patterns.
avoidIf: Photosensitive eye disease, medications that increase photosensitivity, mania risk, or clinician-advised light-therapy avoidance.
implementationNotes: Lifestyle context only; not a sleep-disorder treatment plan.

adjunct.name: Circadian Light Timing
adjunct.description: Consistent wake time, morning light exposure, and evening light reduction as circadian support context.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: AASM and NHLBI resources support appropriately timed light therapy for selected circadian rhythm disorders.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Bright light timing can be counterproductive in some circadian disorders and should be personalized for severe sleep-wake disruption.

citationRefs.title: AASM clinical guideline for circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders
Circadian Rhythm Disorders - Treatment - NHLBI

rationale: DSIP is framed around sleep quality, while light-dark timing is a non-compound driver of circadian alignment.
expectedBenefit: Supports sleep schedule regularity without adding another compound.
safetyCaveats: Incorrect light timing may worsen delayed or non-24 sleep patterns.
avoidIf: Photosensitive eye disease, medications that increase photosensitivity, mania risk, or clinician-advised light-therapy avoidance.
implementationNotes: Lifestyle context only; not a sleep-disorder treatment plan.

adjunct.name: IGF-1 and Glucose Monitoring
adjunct.description: Clinician-managed monitoring context for IGF-1, glucose status, and diabetes risk around GH-axis stimulation.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: Tesamorelin labeling states that it increases IGF-1 and requires periodic IGF-1 monitoring; it also warns about glucose intolerance or diabetes.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Monitoring supports safety context only and should not be interpreted as self-diagnosis or dose adjustment.

citationRefs.title: DailyMed EGRIFTA SV tesamorelin prescribing information

rationale: Tesamorelin stimulates GH and raises IGF-1, and the label highlights IGF-1 and glucose monitoring concerns.
expectedBenefit: Keeps GH-axis benefits paired with explicit clinician-managed monitoring context.
safetyCaveats: Active malignancy, pituitary-axis disease, or glucose intolerance requires clinician oversight.
avoidIf: Active malignancy, pregnancy, pituitary tumor context, or clinician-advised GH-axis avoidance.
implementationNotes: Monitoring context only; not a lab-ordering or dose-adjustment recommendation.

adjunct.name: Semen Analysis and Reproductive Hormone Monitoring
adjunct.description: Clinician-managed semen analysis and reproductive hormone context for fertility-oriented gonadotropin use.
adjunct.evidenceSummary: AUA/ASRM male infertility guidance emphasizes semen analysis and targeted hormonal evaluation in male infertility workups.
adjunct.safetyNotes: Monitoring supports fertility context only and should be managed by a clinician familiar with reproductive endocrinology.

citationRefs.title: Diagnosis and treatment of infertility in men: AUA/ASRM guideline part I
Diagnosis and treatment of infertility in men: AUA/ASRM guideline part II

rationale: HCG is commonly discussed in testosterone and fertility contexts, where semen parameters and hormone status determine whether the goal is being met.
expectedBenefit: Connects gonadotropin use with objective fertility and endocrine monitoring rather than symptom-only tracking.
safetyCaveats: Fertility goals, testicular pain, gynecomastia, abnormal estradiol, or suppressed gonadotropins require clinician review.
avoidIf: Known hormone-sensitive malignancy, unexplained testicular mass, or clinician-advised gonadotropin avoidance.
implementationNotes: Monitoring context only; not a fertility-treatment protocol.
